text: Writing clean code is essential for building software that others can easily understand and modify. One of the most important principles is making your code readable. Use clear naming conventions: choose descriptive names for variables, functions, and classes so that their purpose is obvious. For example, instead of naming a variable "x", call it "userAge". Also, break your code into small, focused functions. Each function should perform one specific task, making testing and debugging much simpler. When you follow these practices, your code becomes more maintainable over time. This allows you and your teammates to update or fix the code efficiently, saving hours of confusion and rework. Clean code is an investment in long-term productivity.
